descriptionLinks: Running Python-based ML workflows in Mat3ra platform: https://docs.mat3ra.com/tutorials/ml/mattersim/
description: How to run Python-based ML workflows in Mat3ra platform.
title: Mat3ra Tutorial: Running Python-based ML workflows in Mat3ra platform

youTubeCaptions.text: Here we will discuss how to run Python-based machine learning models in Matera platform.
The easiest way to get started is by importing an existing workflow from Matera workflow bank.
If the desired workflow is not available, we can create a new workflow and select one of our available ML templates.
If neither the workflow nor the template is found, <break time='0.25'/> we can always start from our general purpose Python workflow and provide our Python code and dependencies.
Now, <break time='0.25'/> let's switch to platform dot matera dot com, and see how these various options work in practice.
First, <break time='0.25'/> let's explore how to import an existing workflow from the bank.
Navigate to the workflow bank and search for 'MatterSim'.
Before we can use these workflows to create a job, we need to copy them to our account collection.
Once copied, <break time='0.25'/> they will appear under the workflows tab.
We can open a workflow to inspect and edit it to our needs.
As we see here, MatterSim total energy workflow consists of three units.
The first unit:  <break time='0.25'/> I O material gets the material data from the job context.
The result is an array of materials.
The second unit: <break time='0.25'/> takes the first item of the 'data' array and assign to another variable named 'material'.
Finally, the MatterSim unit: <break time='0.25'/> uses this material definition to predict its total energy, stress, and other properties.
Besides the main input script, the MatterSim unit also has a utility script and Python requirements file.
We can add additional python packages to the requirements file if needed by our python script.
Once happy with the changes, save and exit the workflow.
Next, navigate to the job designer page, and click 'create' new job <break time='0.25'/> let it be under the default project.
As we see here, the jobs page is consists of three sections: <break time='0.25'/> material, workflow and compute parameters.
For this demonstration, we will select 'nickel' slab as the material.
Then we can select the workflow, which in this case is the 'MatterSim' workflow we just imported.
Let's rename the job to 'MatterSim total energy'.
Depending on our compute needs, we may adjust compute parameters such as cluster, queue, number of processors, time limit, etc.
Finally, save and exit the job designer page.
Now we are ready to submit the job for execution.
Once the job is completed, a summary of output properties will be shown in the 'results' tab.
All input and output files are available under the 'Files' tab. We can preview or download them for further analysis.
Next: <break time='0.25'/> let's move to option two: <break time='0.25'/>this time instead of using a pre-existing workflow, we will create a new workflow.

Let's call it cell relaxation <break time='0.10'/> mattersim, <break time='0.25'/> select Python as application, <break time='0.25'/> add an execution unit, <break time='0.25'/> edit unit, <break time='0.25'/> and select 'MatterSim cell relaxation' flavor <break time='0.25'/> categorized under machine learning force field.

In this case, <break time='0.25'/> we do not want to get our materials information from the job context, <break time='0.25'/> instead we want to define it using 'A S E' library for demonstration purpose.
Here we specify gallium nitride with wurtzite structure.
Once the workflow is created, <break time='0.25'/> we can create and run jobs same way as we did in the previous step.
In this case, <break time='0.25'/> a side by side comparison of initial and relaxed structures is shown in the 'Results' tab.
Initial and final structures are also saved in cif and poscar formats as listed in the 'Files' tab.
Next: <break time='0.15'/> let's discuss how we can run our ML models using GPU acceleration.
This time, <break time='0.15'/>, we create a job using mattersim phonon dispersion workflow.
For debugging purpose, <break time='0.15'/> we print whether the job is running on GPU or CPU.
Most importantly, <break time='0.25'/> in this case, we have to select one of our GPU queues, such as 'G O F' under the compute tab.
Once the job is done, <break time='0.25'/> both phonon dispersion and phonon density of states plots are shown in the 'Results' tab.
We can open the standard output under the 'Files' tab and verify that the job is indeed ran on GPU.
Finally, <break time='0.25'/> let's discuss how we can run any other Python-based machine learning model that is not available in matera platform.

In that case, <break time='0.25'/> we can select our general Python template, <break time='0.25'/> specify all necessary dependencies in the requirements file, <break time='0.25'/> and write our python script accordingly.
As long as our M L model is Python-based, and dependencies can be installed using 'pip', we can run it in Matera platform.
If your model can use multi-threading, you can specify necessary environment variables on top of your python script.
Now, please visit platform dot matera dot com to try it out yourself.
Thank you for watching this tutorial and using Matera platform.
